title: CSRF Protection
description: Master Cross-Site Request Forgery: understand how attackers trick browsers into sending forged requests, and learn to defend with tokens, SameSite cookies, and secure session management.
learning_outcomes: Explain what CSRF is using real-world analogies
Trace a complete CSRF attack step by step
Identify which HTTP methods and actions are vulnerable to CSRF
Implement CSRF token generation using SecureRandom
Store and validate CSRF tokens in server sessions
Embed CSRF tokens in HTML forms as hidden fields
Validate CSRF tokens on every state-changing request
Configure SameSite cookie attributes (Strict, Lax, None)
Check Referer and Origin headers as secondary defence
Build a complete CSRF protection filter for Java servlets
Distinguish CSRF from XSS and understand how they interact

ai_tutor_config.personality: Encouraging, patient, uses real-world analogies. Never judges. Celebrates small wins. Explains CSRF like forged signatures and tricked messengers.

intervention_triggers.idle_40_seconds: CSRF can feel confusing at first because the attack happens invisibly. Think of it like someone forging YOUR signature on a cheque. Want me to walk through the bank analogy again?
intervention_triggers.idle_90_seconds: The key insight is: your browser automatically sends cookies with every request -- even requests triggered by an attacker's page. That is what makes CSRF possible. Shall I draw out the flow?
intervention_triggers.idle_180_seconds: No worries -- this is one of the trickier web attacks to understand. Let me break it into 3 simple steps: (1) You log in, (2) You visit evil page, (3) Evil page uses YOUR session. Want to try a simpler exercise first?
intervention_triggers.first_error: Good attempt! CSRF errors usually come from token mismatches or missing validation. Let me help you trace where the token goes wrong.
intervention_triggers.repeated_errors: CSRF has many moving parts -- sessions, tokens, cookies, forms. Let me simplify: focus ONLY on the token flow first. Generate it, store it, embed it, validate it. One step at a time.
intervention_triggers.after_success: Excellent! You just protected a form against CSRF. Every bank, every social media site, every shopping cart needs exactly this protection. Real-world skill unlocked!

encouragement_phrases: CSRF is the 'invisible attack' -- understanding it puts you ahead of most developers!
You are learning to protect millions of users from having their accounts hijacked.
Every form you protect with a CSRF token is a real defence against real attacks.
Banks, hospitals, and governments all need developers who understand CSRF.
The fact that you can trace this attack flow means you THINK like a security expert.
SameSite cookies are modern defence -- you are learning cutting-edge protection.
Most developers skip CSRF protection. You are not most developers!
Token generation, validation, embedding -- you are building a complete defence system.
Understanding CSRF means understanding how the web REALLY works under the hood.
Every exercise makes you a stronger defender. Keep going!

explanation_styles.analogy: CSRF is like someone forging your signature on a cheque while you are at the bank. You are logged in (the bank knows you), and the forger tricks the bank into processing their cheque because it looks like it came from you.
explanation_styles.visual: Draw three boxes: YOUR BROWSER, EVIL SITE, BANK SERVER. Arrow 1: You log into bank (get cookie). Arrow 2: You visit evil site. Arrow 3: Evil site makes your browser send request to bank WITH your cookie. Bank thinks it is you!
explanation_styles.step_by_step: Step 1: You log into bank.com -- browser stores session cookie. Step 2: You visit evil-site.com in another tab. Step 3: Evil site has hidden form/image that sends request to bank.com. Step 4: Browser automatically attaches your bank cookies. Step 5: Bank processes the request thinking it is from you.
explanation_styles.code_trace: Line 1: Server generates random token (SecureRandom, 32 bytes, Base64). Line 2: Token stored in session. Line 3: Token embedded in form as hidden field. Line 4: On POST, server compares form token with session token. Line 5: Match = legitimate. Mismatch = CSRF attack blocked.
explanation_styles.comparison: XSS = attacker injects script INTO your site. CSRF = attacker uses a DIFFERENT site to trick your browser. XSS steals data. CSRF performs actions. XSS needs an injection point. CSRF needs you to be logged in.

lessons.title: CSRF Protection: From Attack to Defence

theory.introduction:
Imagine you are at your bank, logged in and doing transfers. In another browser tab, you visit a funny memes website. Unknown to you, that memes site has a hidden instruction that tells your browser: 'Send 1000 pounds from this person to account evil37.' Your browser obeys -- because it automatically attaches your bank session cookie to EVERY request to bank.com, even requests triggered by other websites. The bank sees a valid session and processes the transfer. You never clicked anything. You never saw a form. You just lost 1000 pounds.

This is Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF) -- also called 'session riding' or 'one-click attack.' It is consistently in the OWASP Top 10 because it exploits a fundamental feature of how web browsers work: cookies are sent automatically with every request to a domain, regardless of which website initiated the request.

CSRF does not steal your data (that is XSS). Instead, it PERFORMS ACTIONS as you -- transfers money, changes your email, deletes your account, posts on your behalf. The victim is your authenticated session. The weapon is your own browser.

In this course, you will learn exactly how CSRF works, trace real attacks step by step, and build complete defences using tokens, SameSite cookies, and header validation. By the end, you will be able to protect any Java web application against this invisible attack.
theory.why_important: CSRF has been used in real attacks against banks, social media platforms, and government systems. In 2008, a CSRF vulnerability in a major bank allowed attackers to transfer funds from any logged-in customer. Netflix, YouTube, and ING Direct have all had CSRF vulnerabilities. The attack is invisible to the user -- they never see a suspicious form or click a dangerous link. Without CSRF protection, ANY action a logged-in user can perform (transfer money, change password, delete account) can be performed by an attacker through a malicious website. Modern frameworks like Spring Security include CSRF protection by default, but understanding HOW it works is essential for configuring it correctly and protecting custom applications.

key_concepts.concept: What is CSRF? The Forged Cheque Analogy
key_concepts.explanation:
Cross-Site Request Forgery means an attacker tricks your browser into sending a request to a website where you are already logged in. The website cannot tell the difference between a request you intended and one the attacker triggered.

Analogy: You are at the bank (logged in). Someone slips a pre-filled cheque into your pile of paperwork. When the bank processes your papers, it processes the forged cheque too -- because it came from YOUR session at YOUR counter.

Key insight: the browser sends cookies AUTOMATICALLY with every request to a domain. The attacker does not need your password. They just need you to visit their page while logged into the target site.
key_concepts.ai_hint: The attacker never sees your cookie or password. They just trick your browser into using them. It is YOUR browser that betrays you!

key_concepts.explanation:
Step 1 -- LOGIN: You log into bank.com. The server creates a session and sends a session cookie to your browser.

Step 2 -- BAIT: You visit evil-site.com (maybe a link in an email, a forum post, or a meme page). You have NOT logged out of bank.com.

Step 3 -- TRIGGER: The evil page contains hidden HTML that makes your browser send a request to bank.com. This could be an img tag, a hidden form that auto-submits, or JavaScript.

Step 4 -- EXECUTION: Your browser sends the request to bank.com and AUTOMATICALLY attaches the session cookie. The bank sees a valid session and processes the transfer. You never knew it happened.
key_concepts.ai_hint: The attack works because the browser treats bank.com cookies as valid regardless of which page initiated the request. This is how cookies were designed to work!

key_concepts.explanation:
HTTP is stateless -- each request is independent. Cookies solve this by storing a session ID that the browser sends with EVERY request to that domain. This is how 'staying logged in' works.

But this automatic cookie attachment is also what makes CSRF possible. The browser does not ask: 'Did the user intend this request?' It just sends the cookie.

This is NOT a bug in your code. It is how the web was designed. CSRF protection is about adding extra checks that the attacker CANNOT forge.
key_concepts.ai_hint: HttpOnly protects cookies from JavaScript theft (XSS). But HttpOnly does NOT protect against CSRF because the browser sends the cookie regardless.

key_concepts.explanation:
A CSRF token is a random, unpredictable value that the server generates and ties to the user's session. The token is embedded in every form as a hidden field. When the form is submitted, the server checks: does the token in the request match the token in the session?

Why this works: The attacker's evil page can make your browser send cookies (automatic), but the attacker CANNOT read the CSRF token from bank.com's HTML (blocked by Same-Origin Policy). So the attacker cannot include the correct token in their forged request.

The token must be: (1) cryptographically random, (2) unique per session, (3) validated on every state-changing request.
key_concepts.ai_hint: SecureRandom is cryptographically strong. Do NOT use Math.random() or java.util.Random for security tokens -- they are predictable!

key_concepts.explanation:
When a POST request arrives, the server must: (1) Get the token from the session, (2) Get the token from the request parameter, (3) Compare them. If they match, the request is legitimate. If they do not match or the token is missing, reject the request immediately.

Critical: use constant-time comparison to prevent timing attacks. The attacker should learn NOTHING from the rejection -- not which character was wrong, not how long validation took.
key_concepts.ai_hint: MessageDigest.isEqual() does constant-time comparison. Using .equals() would leak timing information that attackers could use to guess the token character by character.

key_concepts.explanation:
SameSite is a cookie attribute that tells the browser: only send this cookie with requests that originate from the SAME site. This directly blocks CSRF because the evil page is a DIFFERENT site.

Three modes:
- Strict: Cookie NEVER sent with cross-site requests. Maximum security but breaks some legitimate flows (e.g., clicking a link from email to a logged-in site).
- Lax: Cookie sent with top-level navigations (clicking links) but NOT with form submissions or AJAX from other sites. Good balance.
- None: Cookie always sent (must use Secure flag). No CSRF protection.

Modern browsers default to Lax if SameSite is not specified.
key_concepts.ai_hint: SameSite=Lax is the sweet spot for most applications. It stops CSRF POST attacks while still allowing users to follow links from emails and other sites.

key_concepts.explanation:
An alternative to server-side token storage. The server sets a random token as BOTH a cookie AND a hidden form field. On submission, the server compares the cookie value with the form value.

Why it works: the attacker can make your browser SEND cookies, but the attacker cannot READ cookies from another domain. So they cannot copy the cookie value into the form field.

Advantage: no server-side state needed (good for stateless/distributed systems). Disadvantage: if the attacker can set cookies (via subdomain or XSS), this pattern is broken.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Double Submit is stateless -- no session storage needed. But the cookie MUST NOT be HttpOnly (JavaScript needs to read it for AJAX requests). Trade-off!

key_concepts.explanation:
The browser sends Referer and Origin headers that tell the server which page initiated the request. Checking these headers provides a secondary defence layer.

Origin header: sent with POST requests, contains just the scheme and domain (https://bank.com). More reliable than Referer.

Referer header: sent with most requests, contains the full URL. Can be stripped by privacy settings.

Limitation: these headers CAN be suppressed (not sent at all) in some situations. So use them as an ADDITIONAL check, not the ONLY defence.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Origin + CSRF token together = defence in depth. If one fails, the other catches the attack.

key_concepts.explanation:
CSRF protection is needed on every STATE-CHANGING action. If a request modifies data, it needs protection.

NEEDS protection: transfers, password changes, email changes, account deletion, posting content, changing settings, admin actions, logout (yes, logout!).

Does NOT need protection: viewing pages, reading data, searching. These are safe because CSRF cannot read the response (Same-Origin Policy blocks it).

Rule of thumb: protect ALL POST, PUT, DELETE, PATCH requests. GET requests should NEVER change state (this is an HTTP specification requirement, not just a security recommendation).
key_concepts.ai_hint: If your GET requests change state, you have TWO problems: (1) HTTP violation, (2) CSRF via img tags. Fix both!

key_concepts.concept: CSRF vs XSS: Understanding the Difference
key_concepts.explanation:
These two attacks are often confused but they are fundamentally different:

XSS (Cross-Site Scripting): Attacker injects malicious script INTO your website. The script runs in the victim's browser with full access to the page. Can steal cookies, modify content, redirect users.

CSRF (Cross-Site Request Forgery): Attacker uses a DIFFERENT website to trick the victim's browser into sending requests. Cannot read responses. Cannot inject code. Can only trigger actions.

Dangerous combination: XSS can DEFEAT CSRF protection! If an attacker has XSS on your site, they can read the CSRF token from the page and include it in their forged request. This is why you need BOTH XSS prevention AND CSRF protection.
key_concepts.ai_hint: XSS is the master key that can unlock CSRF protection. Always fix XSS FIRST, then add CSRF tokens as defence in depth.

key_concepts.explanation:
Rather than adding token checks to every servlet, create a reusable filter that intercepts ALL POST requests and validates the CSRF token automatically.

The filter should: (1) Skip GET/HEAD/OPTIONS requests (read-only), (2) Generate a new token if none exists in the session, (3) Validate the token on every POST/PUT/DELETE, (4) Reject requests with invalid or missing tokens, (5) Make the token available for forms via request attribute.

This is exactly how frameworks like Spring Security implement CSRF protection internally.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Regenerating the token after each valid POST prevents token reuse attacks. This is called 'per-request tokens' vs 'per-session tokens.'

key_concepts.explanation:
CSRF has caused major security incidents:

1. ING Direct (2008): Attackers could transfer money from any logged-in customer by crafting a CSRF request to the transfer endpoint.

2. Netflix (2006): CSRF allowed attackers to change the email address on any logged-in account, then use password reset to take over the account completely.

3. YouTube (2008): Almost all actions were vulnerable to CSRF -- attackers could add videos to favourites, subscribe to channels, and send messages on behalf of victims.

4. Router CSRF: Home routers with default passwords (admin/admin) are attacked via CSRF to change DNS settings, redirecting all traffic through attacker-controlled servers.

These attacks succeeded because the applications trusted that requests with valid session cookies must be intentional.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Router CSRF is especially dangerous because most people never change default router passwords. One malicious web page can compromise an entire network!

key_concepts.explanation:
Modern web apps use AJAX (fetch/XMLHttpRequest) instead of form submissions. CSRF protection for AJAX works differently:

Instead of a hidden form field, the CSRF token is sent as a custom HTTP header (e.g., X-CSRF-Token). The server validates this header.

Why headers work: the Same-Origin Policy prevents JavaScript on evil-site.com from setting custom headers on requests to bank.com. So the attacker cannot add the X-CSRF-Token header.

The token is typically stored in a meta tag on the page or in a cookie that JavaScript can read.
key_concepts.ai_hint: For AJAX apps, the meta tag approach is clean: <meta name="csrf-token" content="TOKEN_HERE">. JavaScript reads it and attaches to every request.

complete_examples.title: Example 1: The Forged Cheque -- CSRF in Plain English
complete_examples.description: Understand CSRF through a real-world analogy with no code
complete_examples.output:
No code output -- this is a conceptual example.
Understand the analogy before moving to code.
complete_examples.ai_explanation: This analogy maps directly to how CSRF works on the web. The badge is the session cookie (sent automatically). The secret stamp is the CSRF token (attacker cannot know it). The key insight: the bank (server) cannot distinguish your forms from the forger's forms without the stamp (token).
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Think of 3 other real-life situations where something similar to CSRF could happen. Hint: anywhere automatic identification is used without verifying intent.

complete_examples.title: Example 2: A Vulnerable Transfer Page (No CSRF Protection)
complete_examples.description: See a complete servlet that is vulnerable to CSRF
complete_examples.output:
When user submits form: Transfer: alice -> friend: $50
When attacker triggers CSRF: Transfer: alice -> evil37: $1000
Both look identical to the server!
complete_examples.ai_explanation: This servlet checks if the user is logged in (session exists) but does NOT verify that the request came from the actual transfer form. Any website can trigger a POST to /transfer, and the browser will include the session cookie automatically. The server has no way to tell legitimate requests from forged ones.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Add a CSRF token to this servlet. You need to: (1) generate a token in doGet, (2) embed it in the form, (3) validate it in doPost.

complete_examples.title: Example 3: The CSRF Attack Page
complete_examples.description: See exactly what the attacker's page looks like
complete_examples.output:
Victim sees: 'Congratulations! You won a free gift card!'
Hidden action: POST to bank.com/transfer?amount=1000&to=evil37
Result: Money transferred without victim knowing
complete_examples.ai_explanation: The attack page looks completely innocent. The form is hidden, the iframe prevents navigation, and the auto-submit happens in milliseconds. The victim never sees the bank page. The key technique: target='hidden' sends the form submission to the invisible iframe, so the page does not navigate away.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Modify this attack to also change the victim's email address on bank.com (POST to /change-email). How would this enable full account takeover?

complete_examples.title: Example 4: CSRF Token Generator (SecureRandom)
complete_examples.description: Build a cryptographically secure token generator
complete_examples.output:
=== CSRF Token Generator ===
Token 1: kX7mP2qR9vL4nW6yB3tF8jH1dA5sC0gE-xZuIoMwKr
  Length: 43 chars
  Entropy: 256 bits
Token 2: (different random string)
  Length: 43 chars
  Entropy: 256 bits
... (all different)

Tokens equal? false
complete_examples.ai_explanation: SecureRandom uses the operating system's cryptographic random number generator (e.g., /dev/urandom on Linux). This produces truly unpredictable bytes. Base64 URL encoding converts binary to safe ASCII characters. 32 bytes = 256 bits of entropy, making brute-force guessing impossible (2^256 possible values).
complete_examples.ai_challenge: What happens if you use java.util.Random instead of SecureRandom? Research how Random's seed makes tokens predictable.

complete_examples.title: Example 5: Complete Protected Transfer Servlet
complete_examples.description: The vulnerable servlet from Example 2, now with full CSRF protection
complete_examples.output:
Legitimate request: Transfer: alice -> friend: $50
CSRF attack: HTTP 403 -- CSRF validation failed
Console: CSRF ATTACK BLOCKED!
complete_examples.ai_explanation: Three changes from the vulnerable version: (1) doGet generates a token and embeds it as a hidden field, (2) doPost validates the token before processing, (3) After successful validation, the token is regenerated (one-time use). The attacker's forged form will not have the correct token because they cannot read it from the real page.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: What happens if the user opens the transfer page in two browser tabs? Both tabs get different tokens but the session stores only the last one. How would you handle this?

complete_examples.title: Example 6: SameSite Cookie Configuration
complete_examples.description: Configure cookies with SameSite attribute for CSRF defence
complete_examples.output:
=== Cookie Security Comparison ===

INSECURE:
Set-Cookie: JSESSIONID=abc123
  - No HttpOnly: XSS can steal it
  - No Secure: sent over HTTP (sniffable)
  - No SameSite: CSRF attacks work

SECURE:
Set-Cookie: JSESSIONID=abc123; Path=/; HttpOnly; Secure; SameSite=Lax; Max-Age=1800
  - HttpOnly: JavaScript cannot read
  - Secure: HTTPS only
  - SameSite=Lax: blocks CSRF POST/AJAX
  - Max-Age: auto-expires in 30 min
complete_examples.ai_explanation: Each cookie flag addresses a different attack: HttpOnly blocks XSS cookie theft, Secure prevents network sniffing, SameSite blocks CSRF, and Max-Age limits the window of opportunity. A cookie without these flags is like a house key left under the doormat.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Change SameSite to Strict and explain what legitimate functionality would break. Then change it to None and explain what security is lost.

complete_examples.title: Example 7: CSRF Attack Detector
complete_examples.description: Build a system that logs and detects CSRF attack attempts
complete_examples.output:
Test 1 (valid): true
[timestamp] CSRF BLOCKED: MISSING_TOKEN from 10.0.0.5 on /transfer
Test 2 (missing): false
[timestamp] CSRF BLOCKED: TOKEN_MISMATCH from 10.0.0.5 on /change-email
Test 3 (wrong): false
[timestamp] CSRF BLOCKED: SESSION_EXPIRED from 10.0.0.5 on /settings
Test 4 (expired): false

=== CSRF Attack Report ===
Total blocked: 3
  [timestamp] CSRF BLOCKED: MISSING_TOKEN from 10.0.0.5 on /transfer
  [timestamp] CSRF BLOCKED: TOKEN_MISMATCH from 10.0.0.5 on /change-email
  [timestamp] CSRF BLOCKED: SESSION_EXPIRED from 10.0.0.5 on /settings
complete_examples.ai_explanation: This detector handles 3 failure modes: missing token (attacker did not include one), wrong token (attacker guessed), and expired session. Logging attack details helps security teams identify patterns -- like repeated attacks from the same IP or targeting the same endpoint.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Add rate limiting: if the same IP triggers 5 CSRF blocks in 10 minutes, automatically block that IP for 1 hour. How would you implement this?

complete_examples.title: Example 8: Double Submit Cookie Implementation
complete_examples.description: Implement the stateless CSRF protection pattern
complete_examples.output:
Generated token: kX7mP2qR9v...

--- Legitimate Request ---
Cookie: csrf=kX7mP2qR9v...
Form:   csrf=kX7mP2qR9v...
Valid: true

--- CSRF Attack ---
Cookie: csrf=kX7mP2qR9v... (sent automatically)
Form:   csrf=??? (attacker cannot read the cookie!)
Valid: false

--- Attack with Fake Token ---
Cookie: csrf=kX7mP2qR9v...
Form:   csrf=aB3nZ8wQ1x...
Valid: false
complete_examples.ai_explanation: Double Submit works because of Same-Origin Policy: JavaScript on evil-site.com can trigger requests to bank.com (cookies sent automatically), but it CANNOT read bank.com's cookies. So the attacker can cause the cookie to be sent but cannot copy its value into the form field.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Research: what happens if the attacker has XSS on a subdomain (e.g., blog.bank.com) and sets a cookie for .bank.com? How does this break Double Submit?

complete_examples.title: Example 9: Origin and Referer Header Validation
complete_examples.description: Check request origin as secondary CSRF defence
complete_examples.output:
=== Origin Validation Tests ===
1. ALLOWED (valid origin: https://mybank.com)
2. BLOCKED (invalid origin: https://evil-site.com)
3. ALLOWED (valid referer)
4. BLOCKED (invalid referer: https://evil-site.com/attack)
5. BLOCKED (no origin or referer)
6. ALLOWED (safe method)
complete_examples.ai_explanation: Origin header is preferred over Referer because: (1) it only contains scheme+domain, not the full URL path (more privacy-friendly), (2) it is more consistently sent with POST requests, (3) it is harder to strip or modify. Referer can be suppressed by the Referrer-Policy header or by navigating from HTTPS to HTTP.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: What should the server do when both Origin and Referer are missing? Some privacy extensions strip these headers. Should you block the request (secure but may break things) or allow it (convenient but less secure)?

complete_examples.title: Example 10: Complete CSRF Filter for Java Servlets
complete_examples.description: A production-ready CSRF filter that protects all endpoints
complete_examples.output:
GET /transfer -> ALLOWED (safe method, token embedded in form)
POST /transfer (valid token) -> ALLOWED (token matches)
POST /transfer (no token) -> 403 CSRF token invalid
POST /transfer (Origin: evil-site.com) -> 403 Invalid origin
AJAX POST with X-CSRF-Token header -> ALLOWED (if token valid)
complete_examples.ai_explanation: This filter implements defence in depth: (1) Safe methods pass through, (2) Origin header checked first, (3) CSRF token validated from either form parameter or custom header (supporting both forms and AJAX), (4) Token regenerated after each valid request. Register this filter in web.xml or with @WebFilter annotation to protect all endpoints automatically.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Add logging to track: (1) how many CSRF attacks are blocked per hour, (2) which endpoints are targeted most, (3) which IPs trigger the most blocks. This data helps identify real attacks vs false positives.

complete_examples.title: Example 11: CSRF in Login Forms (Login CSRF)
complete_examples.description: Understand the less-known login CSRF attack
complete_examples.output:
=== Login CSRF Attack ===

ATTACK STEPS:
1. Attacker creates account: evil@hacker.com / pass123
2. Attacker's page auto-submits login form:
   POST /login
   username=evil@hacker.com&password=pass123
3. Victim is now logged into ATTACKER's account
4. Victim saves credit card -> attacker's account
5. Victim uploads files -> attacker's cloud

DEFENCE:
- Add CSRF token to login form too
- Use pre-session tokens (before login)
- Show logged-in username prominently
- Require re-authentication for sensitive actions
complete_examples.ai_explanation: Login CSRF is counter-intuitive: the attacker logs the victim into the ATTACKER's own account. The danger is that the victim does not realise they are in someone else's account and enters sensitive information. Google, PayPal, and financial institutions all protect their login forms with CSRF tokens.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Design a detection system: how would the application detect that a user is logged into an account from a different device/location than usual?

complete_examples.title: Example 12: Testing for CSRF Vulnerabilities
complete_examples.description: How to check if a web application is vulnerable to CSRF
complete_examples.code:
public class CSRFTestingChecklist {
    
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        System.out.println("=== CSRF Vulnerability Testing Checklist ===");
        System.out.println();
        
        String[] checks = {
            "1. IDENTIFY state-changing endpoints (POST/PUT/DELETE)",
            "   -> Transfer, settings, password change, email change",
            "   -> Delete account, post content, admin actions",
            "",
            "2. CHECK for CSRF tokens in forms",
            "   -> View page source: look for hidden csrf_token field",
            "   -> If no token: VULNERABLE",
            "",
            "3. TEST token validation",
            "   -> Remove token from request -> should get 403",
            "   -> Change one character in token -> should get 403",
            "   -> Use token from different session -> should get 403",
            "   -> If any passes: VULNERABLE",
            "",
            "4. CHECK cookie attributes",
            "   -> SameSite=Lax or Strict? If None: PARTIAL VULN",
            "   -> HttpOnly set? If not: XSS can steal token",
            "   -> Secure set? If not: token exposed on HTTP",
            "",
            "5. TEST with CSRF proof-of-concept",
            "   -> Create HTML page with hidden form to target",
            "   -> Open in browser while logged into target",
            "   -> If action succeeds: CONFIRMED VULNERABLE",
            "",
            "6. CHECK AJAX endpoints",
            "   -> Do they require X-CSRF-Token header?",
            "   -> Can you make requests without custom headers?",
            "   -> CORS policy: is Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * set?",
            "",
            "7. VERIFY Origin/Referer checks",
            "   -> Send POST without Origin header -> should block",
            "   -> Send POST with evil Origin -> should block"
        };
        
        for (String check : checks) {
            System.out.println(check);
        }
    }
}
complete_examples.output:
=== CSRF Vulnerability Testing Checklist ===

1. IDENTIFY state-changing endpoints...
2. CHECK for CSRF tokens in forms...
3. TEST token validation...
4. CHECK cookie attributes...
5. TEST with CSRF proof-of-concept...
6. CHECK AJAX endpoints...
7. VERIFY Origin/Referer checks...
complete_examples.ai_explanation: Security testing follows a systematic approach: identify the attack surface, check for defences, try to bypass them, and verify with a proof of concept. Professional penetration testers use tools like Burp Suite to automate CSRF testing, but understanding the manual process is essential.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Pick any web application you use daily. Without doing anything malicious, view the page source on a form page. Can you find a CSRF token? What is it called? Is it in a hidden field or a meta tag?

complete_examples.title: Example 13: CSRF Protection in REST APIs
complete_examples.description: How CSRF applies to modern API-based applications
complete_examples.output:
=== REST API CSRF Analysis ===

COOKIE AUTH (vulnerable to CSRF):
  Request: POST /api/transfer
  Cookie: session=abc123  <- sent automatically!
  CSRF possible: YES

BEARER TOKEN AUTH (immune to CSRF):
  Request: POST /api/transfer
  Authorization: Bearer eyJhbGci...  <- NOT automatic!
  CSRF possible: NO

MIXED AUTH (common in SPAs):
  Both needed: cookie (auto) + token (manual)
  CSRF possible: NO
complete_examples.ai_explanation: Bearer token auth is inherently CSRF-proof because the token must be explicitly added by JavaScript. However, if the bearer token is stored in a cookie (instead of localStorage), CSRF becomes possible again. The storage location matters!
complete_examples.ai_challenge: If a Single Page App stores the JWT in localStorage, is it safe from CSRF? What about XSS? Which storage method (cookie vs localStorage) is better and why?

complete_examples.title: Example 14: Defence in Depth -- Combining All Protections
complete_examples.description: Build a comprehensive CSRF defence strategy
complete_examples.output:
=== CSRF Defence in Depth ===

LAYER 1: SameSite Cookies
  Blocks: POST/AJAX from other sites

LAYER 2: CSRF Tokens
  Blocks: All CSRF (attacker cannot read token)

LAYER 3: Origin/Referer Checks
  Blocks: Cross-origin requests

LAYER 4: Re-authentication
  Blocks: Even if CSRF succeeds, attacker needs password

COMBINED: All 4 layers must be bypassed for attack.
complete_examples.ai_explanation: No single defence is perfect. SameSite fails on old browsers. Tokens fail if XSS exists. Headers can be stripped. But combining all four means an attacker must bypass EVERY layer simultaneously -- which is practically impossible. This is the principle of Defence in Depth applied to CSRF.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: For a banking application, rank these 4 layers from most important to least important. Justify your ranking.

visual_diagram.title: CSRF Attack and Defence Flow
visual_diagram.content: CSRF ATTACK FLOW STEP 1: LOGIN User logs into bank.com Browser stores session cookie | v STEP 2: BAIT User visits evil-site.com (email link, ad, forum) User has NOT logged out of bank.com | v STEP 3: TRIGGER Evil page contains hidden form/img/script Auto-submits request to bank.com THREE attack methods: a) <img src="bank.com/xfer?amt=1000"> b) Hidden form with auto-submit c) XMLHttpRequest / fetch() | v STEP 4: EXECUTION Browser sends request to bank.com Cookie attached AUTOMATICALLY Bank sees valid session -- processes transfer VICTIM LOSES MONEY! CSRF DEFENCE FLOW CHECK 1: SameSite Cookie Is request from same site? YES --> Continue to Check 2 NO --> BLOCK (cookie not sent) | v CHECK 2: Origin Header Does Origin match our domain? YES --> Continue to Check 3 NO --> BLOCK (wrong origin) | v CHECK 3: CSRF Token Does form token match session token? YES --> ALLOW REQUEST NO --> BLOCK (invalid token) CSRF vs XSS COMPARISON CSRF XSS Tricks browser Injects code From DIFFERENT site Runs ON your site Cannot read responses Full page access Performs ACTIONS Steals DATA Defence: tokens + SameSite Defence: output encoding WARNING: XSS can bypass CSRF tokens! REMEMBER 1. CSRF = attacker uses YOUR session via YOUR browser 2. Cookies are sent AUTOMATICALLY -- that is the problem 3. CSRF tokens: generate (SecureRandom) -> store (session) -> embed (form) -> validate (POST) 4. SameSite=Lax blocks most CSRF (modern browsers) 5. Never use GET for state-changing actions 6. Defence in depth: tokens + SameSite + Origin check + re-auth 7. XSS defeats CSRF -- fix XSS first!

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 1: What Makes CSRF Possible?
practice_exercises.description: Fill in the blanks to explain why CSRF works.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Let us start with the fundamentals -- WHY does CSRF work?
practice_exercises.expected_output:
CSRF works because: cookies are sent automatically
Attacker can see cookie: no
Attacker can use cookie: yes
Safe method: GET

hints.message: Think about what your browser does when you visit any website. What gets attached to the request?
Cookies are the answer to blank1. The key insight: the attacker cannot READ cookies but CAN make the browser SEND them.
blank1="cookies"; blank2="no"; blank3="yes"; blank4="GET"; -- The paradox of CSRF: attacker cannot see the cookie but can use it!

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 2: Match the Attack to the Defence
practice_exercises.description: Connect each CSRF attack method to its defence.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Different attacks need different defences. Let us match them up!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Attack: Hidden form auto-submits to bank.com
Defence: csrf_token

Attack: img tag triggers GET request
Defence: never use GET for state changes

Attack: AJAX POST from evil-site.com
Defence: samesite_cookie + origin_check

Attack: CSRF on password change page
Defence: re_authentication

Attack: Cross-origin form submission
Defence: csrf_token + samesite_cookie

hints.message: Each attack exploits a different weakness. Think about what each defence prevents.
Hidden forms need tokens. GET transfers need HTTP method fix. AJAX needs origin checks. Password changes need re-auth.
defences = {"csrf_token", "never use GET for state changes", "samesite_cookie + origin_check", "re_authentication", "csrf_token + samesite_cookie"}

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 3: Identify the Vulnerable Code
practice_exercises.description: Find the CSRF vulnerability in this servlet.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Can you spot what is missing from this code?
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Missing protection 1: No CSRF token validation
Missing protection 2: No Origin/Referer header check
Missing protection 3: No SameSite cookie attribute

hints.message: The code checks if the user is logged in, but does it check if the REQUEST was intentional?
Three things are missing: (1) token check, (2) header check, (3) cookie attribute. The session check only confirms WHO, not WHETHER they intended the action.
println("Missing protection 1: No CSRF token validation"); println("Missing protection 2: No Origin/Referer header check"); println("Missing protection 3: No SameSite cookie attribute");

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 4: Trace the CSRF Attack
practice_exercises.description: Step through a CSRF attack and predict what happens at each stage.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Let us walk through the attack step by step!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
=== CSRF Attack Trace ===

Step 1: Alice logs into bank.com
  Browser receives: Set-Cookie: JSESSIONID=abc123

Step 2: Alice visits evil-memes.com
  Hidden on page: <form action='bank.com/transfer' method='POST'>...

Step 3: Browser sends request to bank.com
  Includes: Cookie: JSESSIONID=abc123 (automatic!)

Step 4: Bank server receives request
  Bank sees: valid session for Alice
  Bank does: processes transfer to attacker's account

Step 5: Result
  Alice: knows nothing -- still looking at memes
  Attacker: received $1000 from Alice

hints.message: At Step 1, what does the browser store? At Step 3, what does the browser automatically attach?
The cookie (JSESSIONID) is the key. The browser sends it with ALL requests to bank.com, even from evil-memes.com.
Step 1: Set-Cookie: JSESSIONID=abc123. Step 2: hidden form to bank.com. Step 3: Cookie auto-attached. Step 4: valid session, processes transfer. Step 5: Alice unaware, attacker gets money.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 5: CSRF Quiz: True or False
practice_exercises.description: Test your understanding of CSRF fundamentals.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Quick knowledge check -- true or false?
practice_exercises.expected_output:
1. CSRF can steal the victim's password: false
2. CSRF requires the victim to be logged in: true
3. SSL/HTTPS prevents CSRF: false
4. CSRF can work via an img tag: true
5. SameSite=Strict blocks all CSRF: true
6. The attacker needs the victim's cookie value: false
7. GET requests should change state: false
8. CSRF tokens must be cryptographically random: true

hints.message: CSRF performs actions, it does not steal data. Think about what the attacker CAN and CANNOT do.
Key facts: CSRF cannot read responses (so cannot steal passwords). It needs an active session. HTTPS encrypts the channel but does not prevent CSRF.
1=false, 2=true, 3=false, 4=true (GET request via img src), 5=true, 6=false (browser sends it automatically), 7=false, 8=true (predictable tokens can be guessed)

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 6: Classify HTTP Methods
practice_exercises.description: Mark each as NEEDS_CSRF or NO_CSRF protection.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Remember: state-changing methods need protection!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
GET /profile -> NO_CSRF
POST /transfer -> NEEDS_CSRF
DELETE /account -> NEEDS_CSRF
GET /search -> NO_CSRF
PUT /settings -> NEEDS_CSRF
POST /login -> NEEDS_CSRF
GET /image.jpg -> NO_CSRF
POST /change-email -> NEEDS_CSRF

hints.message: GET = read-only = safe. POST/PUT/DELETE = state-changing = needs protection.
Even login needs CSRF protection (Login CSRF attack).
answers = {"NO_CSRF","NEEDS_CSRF","NEEDS_CSRF","NO_CSRF","NEEDS_CSRF","NEEDS_CSRF","NO_CSRF","NEEDS_CSRF"}

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 7: Spot the CSRF Attack HTML
practice_exercises.description: Which HTML snippets are CSRF attacks?
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Attackers hide CSRF in innocent-looking HTML!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
1. CSRF_ATTACK
2. SAFE
3. CSRF_ATTACK
4. SAFE
5. CSRF_ATTACK

hints.message: CSRF triggers unwanted requests. Regular links and local images are safe.
img with bank URL = GET CSRF. Hidden form = POST CSRF. iframe with action URL = CSRF.
answers = {"CSRF_ATTACK","SAFE","CSRF_ATTACK","SAFE","CSRF_ATTACK"}

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 8: Cookie Attributes Quiz
practice_exercises.description: Match each cookie attribute to what it protects against.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Each cookie flag stops a different attack!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
HttpOnly  -> protects against: XSS cookie theft
Secure    -> protects against: network sniffing
SameSite  -> protects against: CSRF
Max-Age   -> protects against: session hijacking (limits time window)

hints.message: Each flag addresses a specific attack vector.
HttpOnly = JS cannot read. Secure = HTTPS only. SameSite = same origin only.
HttpOnly->XSS cookie theft, Secure->sniffing, SameSite->CSRF, Max-Age->long-lived sessions

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 9: Why Doesn't HTTPS Stop CSRF?
practice_exercises.description: Explain why encryption does not prevent CSRF.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: This is a common misconception. Let us clear it up!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
HTTPS provides: encrypted communication channel
HTTPS does NOT prevent: forged requests from other sites
Reason: CSRF does not need to read the traffic -- it just sends requests

Analogy: HTTPS is like a secure phone line -- it prevents eavesdropping, but it cannot stop someone from dialling your number and placing a fake order

hints.message: HTTPS protects the CHANNEL. CSRF abuses the SESSION. Different things!
The attacker does not need to intercept anything. They just trigger a request.
HTTPS=encrypted channel. CSRF=forged request using valid session. HTTPS cannot tell if a request was intended by the user or triggered by an attacker.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 10: CSRF Impact Assessment
practice_exercises.description: Rate the severity of CSRF on different endpoints.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Not all CSRF attacks are equally dangerous!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
POST /change-theme -> LOW
POST /transfer-money -> CRITICAL
POST /change-password -> CRITICAL
POST /change-email -> HIGH
POST /add-to-cart -> LOW
DELETE /delete-account -> CRITICAL
POST /post-comment -> MEDIUM
POST /admin/create-user -> CRITICAL

hints.message: Think about what the attacker gains from each action.
Money transfers and credential changes are critical. Theme changes are low impact.
change-theme=LOW, transfer=CRITICAL, change-password=CRITICAL, change-email=HIGH (enables account takeover), add-to-cart=LOW, delete-account=CRITICAL, post-comment=MEDIUM, admin-create-user=CRITICAL

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 11: Generate a CSRF Token
practice_exercises.description: Write a method that generates a cryptographically secure CSRF token.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Time to build CSRF token logic!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Token: (43 random characters)
Length: 43
Different each time: true

hints.message: Start with SecureRandom random = new SecureRandom();
byte[] bytes = new byte[32]; random.nextBytes(bytes); then Base64.getUrlEncoder().withoutPadding().encodeToString(bytes)
static String generateToken() { SecureRandom r = new SecureRandom(); byte[] b = new byte[32]; r.nextBytes(b); return Base64.getUrlEncoder().withoutPadding().encodeToString(b); }

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 12: Store Token in Session
practice_exercises.description: Simulate storing a CSRF token in a user session.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Time to build CSRF token logic!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Stored: abc123secure
Has token: true
After clear: null

hints.message: Use session.put("csrf_token", token) to store.
getToken returns session.get("csrf_token"). clearToken uses session.remove("csrf_token").
static void storeToken(String t){session.put("csrf_token",t);} static String getToken(){return session.get("csrf_token");} static void clearToken(){session.remove("csrf_token");}

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 13: Validate CSRF Token
practice_exercises.description: Write constant-time token comparison.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Time to build CSRF token logic!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Match: true
Mismatch: false
Null form: false
Null session: false
Both null: false

hints.message: First check for nulls, then use MessageDigest.isEqual().
if (sessionToken == null || formToken == null) return false; then MessageDigest.isEqual(sessionToken.getBytes(), formToken.getBytes())
static boolean validateToken(String s, String f){if(s==null||f==null)return false; return MessageDigest.isEqual(s.getBytes(),f.getBytes());}

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 14: Build Hidden Form Field
practice_exercises.description: Generate HTML for a CSRF token hidden field.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Time to build CSRF token logic!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
<input type="hidden" name="csrf_token" value="abc123">
<input type="hidden" name="csrf_token" value="token-with-quotes"">

hints.message: String concatenation with the token value embedded.
Escape quotes: token.replace("\"", """) to prevent HTML injection.
static String generateHiddenField(String t){String safe=t.replace("\"","""); return "<input type=\"hidden\" name=\"csrf_token\" value=\""+safe+"\">";}
